topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail arrived, a thick, glossy brochure tucked into the usual stack. Mark barely glanced at it, his eyes drifting back to the half-finished crossword puzzle spread across his coffee table. He swiped the paper with a careless finger, the movement slow and deliberate. When he finally unfolded it, the words swam before his eyes: "Building Transformation: Preparing for Condominium Conversion." A low groan escaped him, a sound of profound inconvenience. He let the brochure fall back onto the pile, the weight of the impending change settling heavily on his shoulders. Why bother unpacking? Why bother cleaning? He just wanted the quiet inertia to continue, undisturbed by any forced reorganization.

[story 2]

Sarah stared blankly at the eviction notice tacked to the community board. The fine print, detailing the mandatory move-out dates and the impending shift to ownership structures, seemed to blur into meaningless shapes. She leaned her head back against the peeling wallpaper, a heavy sigh escaping her lips. The thought of coordinating movers, sorting through years of accumulated possessions, felt like an insurmountable mountain. Her limbs felt weighted, each movement requiring an unnecessary expenditure of energy. "Great," she mumbled to the empty room, picking up a remote control and dropping it without looking at the television. It was easier to just let things remain exactly as they were, suspended in their current state of disarray.

[story 3]

When the developer’s announcement finally reached the building, David simply blinked. He was halfway through a very long nap, his head resting precariously on a stack of outdated magazines. The news registered as a dull thud rather than a jolt. He slowly lifted his head, the movement stiff, and squinted at the printed notice taped to the elevator bank. A faint sense of resistance washed over him, a deep-seated desire to avoid any form of strenuous activity. He let his gaze drift away, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the sliver of afternoon light, perfectly content to let the logistics of the transition unfold without his direct involvement.
